methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $37M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
EX-Fusion is a Series A nuclear energy company founded by Kazuki Matsuo and Shinsuke Fujioka that has raised $37M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparative universe of 11 peers across nuclear energy—3 public companies (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private firms including Fuse Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Oklo—though a sufficiently robust same-stage peer subset was not available.

Stage-progression methodology is the applicable triangulation approach, using the $37M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A progression to subsequent funding rounds. This yields an implied valuation of $74M. No peer-median analysis could be performed; while 8 same-sector private peers exist, filtering for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier comparables did not yield the minimum 3 datapoints required for statistical confidence. A sector-wide median would introduce unacceptable bias given the heterogeneity of valuation stages and capital efficiency across nuclear subsectors.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $63M (low) to $85M (high), with a midpoint of $74M. This range reflects modest variance around the stage-progression result given the single-method constraint. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-peer validation, making the 2.0x multiplier assumption the critical driver; near-term funding trajectory and burn rate relative to milestones would materially inform stage-to-stage step-up appropriateness.
